description: The GPIO functional clock is disabled after reset and must be explicitly enabled before any register access. The enable sequence uses the Power and Sleep Controller to transition the GPIO Local PSC (LPSC) to ENABLE. Software programs MDCTL, triggers PTCMD, and polls PTSTAT until the transition completes, then verifies MDSTAT. The GPIO clock derives from the CPU clock and is documented as CPU divided by six for this device. Failing to enable the PSC will leave GPIO registers inactive and reads or writes may not take effect.
evidence: DSP Subsystem PSC User’s Guide: MDCTL, PDCTL, PTCMD, PTSTAT, MDSTAT; C6452 note: GPIO clock = CPU/6
dependencies: Power domain ON; PSC configured; LPSC for GPIO set to ENABLE
Remarks: Execute full PSC state transition and verify MDSTAT before configuring GPIO; guard init with a ready flag and retry logic.

feature_name: Pin multiplexing selection for GPIO pins
description: Many package pins are multiplexed and are not routed to GPIO by default. Software must program the device pin multiplexing registers to select the GPIO function for each desired pin. The default reset configuration may map pins to alternate peripherals, so pinmux must be configured before direction or data settings. Incorrect pinmux leaves GPIO logic internal and external pins unresponsive. Board-specific constraints should be validated during bring-up and captured in configuration tables.
evidence: SPRS371 (C6452 Data Manual) Pin Multiplexing chapter and pinmux control registers
dependencies: Pinmux control registers; board schematic; PSC enabled for pinmux domain
Remarks: Centralize pinmux writes and keep them adjacent to GPIO init; verify no conflicts with other peripherals before enabling edges.

description: Each GPIO signal is individually configurable as input or output. The DIR register provides per-bit selection where bit n controls the direction of GP[n]. Software must observe the documented polarity of the bits to avoid inverted configuration. Direction should be established after enabling the clock and selecting pinmux for the targeted pins. Runtime changes should be sequenced with safe output defaults to avoid unintended toggles.
evidence: SPRUF95 (GPIO User’s Guide): DIR register description
dependencies: GPIO clock enabled; pinmux selected for GPIO; stable external conditions
Remarks: Wrap direction changes with helper APIs and predrive safe levels; confirm polarity from the manual on each silicon revision.

description: The controller offers atomic bit manipulation to drive outputs without read‑modify‑write hazards. Writing to SET_DATA asserts selected output bits high and writing to CLR_DATA drives them low. These registers ensure concurrent software or interrupt activity does not corrupt neighboring bits. Applications should prefer these atomic paths instead of full‑port OUTDATA writes for partial updates. Using atomic accesses improves ISR safety and reduces the need for long critical sections.
evidence: SPRUF95: SET_DATA and CLR_DATA register behavior
dependencies: DIR configured as output; GPIO clock enabled
Remarks: Use SET_DATA and CLR_DATA for single‑bit or masked updates; batch operations to minimize bus transactions and jitter.

description: The OUTDATA register mirrors the output port and may permit read and write of the entire bitmap. Partial updates using OUTDATA can introduce read‑modify‑write races if interrupts also manipulate bits. Atomic SET/CLR accesses are strongly preferred for robustness in concurrent contexts. If OUTDATA writes are used, they should be confined to fully managed critical sections with interrupts masked. Reading OUTDATA can be useful for state snapshots when synchronized with application logic.
evidence: SPRUF95: OUTDATA register notes and atomic access recommendation
dependencies: Coordinated access discipline; interrupts masked or atomic ops used
Remarks: Avoid OUTDATA for partial changes; if required, protect with locking or brief interrupt masking to prevent lost updates.

description: Input levels are captured through synchronizing flip‑flops to mitigate metastability on asynchronous signals. The INDATA register exposes the logic level observed by the GPIO block after synchronization. Software can poll INDATA to read pin states independent of output settings on other pins. Sampling should consider external signal timing and the small synchronization latency. Masked reads enable efficient acquisition of multiple inputs in a single transaction.
evidence: SPRUF95: INDATA register and input synchronization path
dependencies: GPIO clock enabled; DIR configured for input on targeted bits
Remarks: Cache masks for polled inputs and rate‑limit reads; add external filtering or debouncing for noisy sources when required.

description: Per‑pin rising‑edge detection can be enabled to trigger events or interrupts on positive transitions. Software enables detection by writing ones to the SET_RIS_TRIG register for target bits. Disabling is performed by writing ones to the CLR_RIS_TRIG counterpart for the same bits. Proper initialization requires clearing any stale latched status before enabling new edges to avoid spurious ISRs. Combining with falling‑edge detection allows coverage of both transitions on toggling signals.
evidence: SPRUF95: SET_RIS_TRIG and CLR_RIS_TRIG registers
dependencies: Pin configured and stable; interrupt routing prepared; status cleared
Remarks: Enable only necessary edges to reduce interrupt rate; document chosen polarity and expected activity per signal.

description: Per‑pin falling‑edge detection can be enabled to trigger events or interrupts on negative transitions. The SET_FAL_TRIG register turns on falling‑edge latching for selected bits under software control. Disabling is managed via the CLR_FAL_TRIG register by writing ones to the corresponding bits. As with rising edges, software should clear any prior latched events before activation to prevent false triggers. Correct configuration ensures deterministic response to negative transitions on external lines.
evidence: SPRUF95: SET_FAL_TRIG and CLR_FAL_TRIG registers
dependencies: Pin configured and stable; interrupt routing prepared; status cleared
Remarks: Balance rising and falling enables based on expected duty cycle and noise; consider source deglitching for fast edges.

description: The controller latches edge events into a per‑pin status register for diagnostic and ISR use. Software reads the status to identify source pins and clears serviced events by writing ones to the corresponding bits. This write‑one‑to‑clear behavior prevents accidental clearing of unrelated sources during concurrent handling. Bank‑level status can be read first to narrow candidates before per‑pin reads for efficiency. Correct clearing on both GPIO and CPU sides is essential to avoid missed or repeated interrupts.
evidence: SPRUF95: INSTAT (R/W1C) and INTSTAT01 bank status
dependencies: Interrupt or event enabled; ISR path established; CPU flags handled
Remarks: Follow a fixed ISR order: read, act, clear GPIO status, then clear CPU IFR; test race scenarios during verification.

description: GPIO pins are organized into banks with aggregated interrupt lines for reduced CPU vector usage. When a bank interrupt fires, software consults the bank status register to locate the active bank and then drills down to per‑pin status to find exact sources. This scheme preserves per‑bit visibility while minimizing interrupt inputs to the CPU. Multiple pins can assert simultaneously and must be serviced comprehensively within the ISR. Robust handlers iterate all set bits and recheck status until no sources remain.
evidence: SPRUF95: INTSTAT01 and banking description
dependencies: Interrupt mux configured; bank line connected to CPU INTx
Remarks: Design ISRs to iterate bitfields atomically and handle coalesced events; document bank‑to‑pin mapping in headers.

description: The device supports per‑GPIO or banked interrupt outputs toward the CPU fabric depending on configuration. In direct modes, individual GPIOs can map to distinct CPU interrupt lines for precise handling and simpler identification. Availability and exact counts are device‑specific and governed by the interrupt mapping tables for the C6452. Software should choose between direct and banked modes based on vector availability and latency needs. Verification should confirm the final mapping in both the mux and CPU IER/IFR registers.
evidence: SPRUF95 and device interrupt mapping: per‑pin vs banked availability
dependencies: Interrupt multiplexor configured; available CPU INTx lines; IER/IFR managed
Remarks: Prefer direct interrupts for latency‑critical pins; use banked aggregation where vector resources are constrained.

description: GPIO interrupt outputs must be routed through the device interrupt mux to the chosen CPU vector. Software programs the mux, enables the selected INTx in the CPU IER, and manages IFR clearing during service. The routing strategy directly impacts latency and priority among competing sources in the system. Incorrect mux configuration leaves interrupts pending but unserviced and can stall flows. Validation should observe IFR bits and exercise test pulses on inputs to confirm end‑to‑end paths.
evidence: Device interrupt mux/control; CPU IER/IFR (SPRU732)
dependencies: Interrupt mux registers; CPU IER enable; IFR service in ISR
Remarks: Capture mux setup in a single function paired with ISR installation; add self‑tests that pulse known bits at startup.

feature_name: EDMA event generation from GPIO edges
description: Selected GPIO edge events can be routed to the EDMA controller for autonomous transfers. This enables hardware‑triggered data movement or timestamp capture without CPU polling overhead. Proper configuration involves mapping GPIO events to EDMA channels via the event crossbar for the device. Software must ensure edge polarity and expected rate align with EDMA trigger capabilities and queue priorities. Misconfiguration can cause missed transfers or excessive bursts that impact system bandwidth.
evidence: Device event mapping tables; EDMA crossbar configuration references
dependencies: EDMA controller present; event crossbar configured; GPIO edge enables
Remarks: Use EDMA for high‑frequency edges; throttle with edge filters and verify channel priorities to avoid bus contention.

description: Incoming GPIO signals pass through synchronization logic before sampling by the GPIO core. This introduces a small latency that stabilizes readings against metastability and short glitches. The behavior becomes important when measuring very short pulses or edge‑to‑ISR timing. Debug sessions that halt the core may change the timing relationship to the synchronizers and skew observations. Software should account for this difference between in‑circuit operation and halted emulation.
evidence: SPRUF95: Synchronization stage prior to INDATA sampling
dependencies: GPIO clock stable; external signal integrity
Remarks: Document propagation delay through the synchronizers and add hardware deglitching for very narrow pulses if needed.

description: Each GPIO register bit maps directly to the same‑numbered GP pin for clarity. The device organizes pins into banks to simplify aggregation and interrupt wiring. This one‑to‑one bit mapping enables mask‑based operations and improves code readability across the driver. Software should use constants or macros to represent pins and banks consistently across modules. Clear mapping reduces errors when ports are repurposed or shared across boards and configurations.
evidence: SPRUF95: Bit n corresponds to GP[n]; bank organization details
dependencies: None beyond GPIO enable and pinmux
Remarks: Define symbolic names for pins and banks in a central header; keep mapping tables version‑controlled with board revisions.

description: A recommended sequence brings the module from reset to operational state reliably. First ensure the power domain is on and enable the GPIO LPSC via the PSC sequence with status verification. Next configure pin multiplexing so desired pins are visible to the GPIO logic before touching DIR. Program DIR for inputs and outputs and set initial output levels atomically with SET/CLR to avoid glitches. Finally, clear stale edge status and enable required edge detections and routes through the interrupt mux.
evidence: SPRUF95 and PSC User’s Guide initialization steps; Pinmux chapter in SPRS371
dependencies: PSC enabled; pinmux programmed; DIR set; status cleared
Remarks: Provide an idempotent init routine with read‑backs and asserts; log failures and block access until ready is true.

feature_name: Concurrent updates and RMW hazard avoidance
description: Concurrent access by threads or ISRs can corrupt OUTDATA‑based partial writes under interrupt load. Using atomic SET_DATA and CLR_DATA avoids read‑modify‑write hazards entirely for single‑bit or masked updates. Any unavoidable full‑port operations should be protected with critical sections to serialize access safely. Group atomic writes to minimize bus cycles and jitter in time‑sensitive paths. This practice improves determinism and simplifies reasoning about concurrency in the driver.
evidence: SPRUF95: Recommendation to prefer SET/CLR over OUTDATA for atomicity
dependencies: SET_DATA and CLR_DATA available; ISR design aware of access patterns
Remarks: Audit all write paths and replace partial OUTDATA sequences; add tests to cover simultaneous ISR and task updates.

description: Latched edge status persists while the core is halted during debugging sessions. Stepping through ISRs without proper clearing can lead to repeated servicing of the same event on resume. Developers should explicitly clear GPIO INSTAT bits and CPU IFR flags when single‑stepping or exiting halt states. Timing‑sensitive measurements may not reflect real‑time behavior while the core is stopped. Add debug‑only assertions to verify clear‑and‑ack order in development builds.
evidence: SPRUF95: Emulation and debug considerations; CPU IFR handling (SPRU732)
dependencies: Debugger attached; interrupts masked appropriately during step
Remarks: Provide a debug helper to clear status registers and CPU flags; document non‑real‑time behavior during halt states.

description: After device or module reset, GPIO registers return to documented defaults per the user’s guide. The functional clock is disabled and pinmux selections revert to reset mapping that may disconnect GPIOs. Software must reapply the full initialization sequence to restore operation before accessing GPIO registers. Stale edge statuses should be cleared before enabling detection to prevent spurious interrupts on startup. This guarantees a clean baseline and consistent behavior across cold and warm boots.
evidence: SPRUF95: Reset behavior for GPIO registers; default power state
dependencies: PSC enable; pinmux restore; status clear; DIR reprogrammed
Remarks: Call the canonical init on every boot path; detect unexpected reset states and log for field diagnostics.

description: At power‑up the GPIO module is clock‑gated and inactive by default as part of low‑power design. Register writes may appear to succeed but have no external effect until the clock is enabled through the PSC. Reads can return reset values that do not reflect external pins prior to enabling the module. Enabling the PSC and verifying module state is mandatory before use to avoid misinterpretation. This behavior is typical of clock‑gated SoC peripherals and should be documented in bring‑up checklists.
evidence: Device default power/clock state note; PSC User’s Guide
dependencies: PSC sequence completed; domain ON; MDSTAT verified
Remarks: Gate all GPIO accesses behind a checked ready flag set only after PSC enable; add error handling for failed transitions.
